title: Repair After a Mistake
summary: Take responsibility and make things right after making an error.
learnerPromise: You will be able to face a mistake, take responsibility, and do something to make the situation better.
whyItMatters: Everyone makes mistakes. Knowing how to repair the situation helps you learn, maintains trust with others, and keeps a small error from becoming a bigger problem.
realLifeUses: You forget to return a borrowed item and reach out to apologize and arrange to return it
You miss a deadline and notify the person, explain briefly, and offer a new time
You say something hurtful and later apologize and ask how you can make it right
You make an error on a shared document and fix it while letting others know what happened
materialsNeeded: A way to communicate with the person affected (in person, message, or call)
A few minutes to think before acting
result: A clear plan or action that addresses the mistake and moves toward making it right.
youWillLearn: How to admit a mistake without making excuses
How to offer a genuine, brief apology
How to suggest or ask what would help fix the situation
How to move forward after repairing so you do not dwell on the error
miniChallenge: Think of a small mistake you made recently. Write down one sentence admitting it and one sentence offering to make it right. If you feel ready, share it with the person involved.
steps: Pause and admit to yourself what happened — no excuses, just facts
Think about who was affected and what they might need
Reach out with a brief, clear message: name the mistake and apologize
Offer to fix it or ask what you can do to make things right
Follow through on what you offered, then let yourself move on
completionCriteria: You wrote or spoke an honest acknowledgment of the mistake
You offered or did something to repair the situation
commonProblems: You might want to explain why it happened — keep explanations very brief
You may feel embarrassed — remember that repairing shows maturity
The other person might still be upset — your job is to repair, not to control their reaction
tips: A good repair is short, honest, and focused on action — not a long story
Do not over-apologize. One sincere apology and a repair action is enough
If you cannot fix the mistake directly, ask sincerely what the other person needs
